caveats: a: Occupation evidence is for NAICS 445 or national occupations, not 445291 specifically.
a: Self-checkout is conventional automation and is excluded except where new AI changes residual work.
a: The frozen compensation ratio has a 2024-wage/2022-receipts vintage mismatch and is harmonized from an unadjusted six-digit input.
rho: BTOS is sector-wide and measures any AI use, not the share of exposed labor opportunity implemented.
rho: Implementation may improve service or sales without reducing labor, which is not counted here.
e: No published source measures the recurring external-customer service share inside 445291.
e: The frozen n=11 is a margin-bridged estimate from size-class firm counts and average receipts, not an observed EBITDA-band census.
s5: The source covers all US businesses and a ten-year need-for-buyer concept, not completed five-year control transfers.
s5: Few eligible firms are implied after applying e to the frozen n, so one transaction would change the realized rate sharply.
q: No source directly observes benefit sharing following AI implementation in 445291.
q: The estimate excludes volume loss, which is handled in demand and operator-required share.
d5: BLS projections are employment measures and cover broader populations than 445291 demand.
d5: Constant-quality product mix is difficult to distinguish from premiumization and package-size changes.
o: The estimate distinguishes elimination of the lens operator from digitization of customer interaction.
o: A channel shift can preserve baked-goods demand while removing the dedicated 445291 operator.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The eligible model earns predominantly fixed-price product revenue from repeated orders or scheduled deliveries to external customers, with merchandising, ordering, fulfillment, and account service embedded in the product margin.
lens.excluded_activities: Walk-in consumer-only shops without a recurring outsourced supply relationship; products baked on premises; bakery cafes and immediate-consumption food service; captive outlets, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US baked-goods retailers selling products made off premises through recurring or repeat supply relationships, such as standing business, institutional, hospitality, or event accounts, within the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: BLS reports that cashiers, stock clerks/order fillers, retail supervisors, and food-preparation workers are common across the broader NAICS 445 subsector, while O*NET lists transaction processing, records, customer questions, inventory, and purchasing among cashier and retail-sales tasks. I map the digital information portions of those occupations, plus routine scheduling and promotion work, to 445291 and exclude physical stocking, packing, delivery, and already-automated checkout. The broader food-retail mix likely overstates exposure if eligible specialty shops rely more on relationship selling, but may understate it where LMM firms have centralized purchasing and administration.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for NAICS 445 or national occupations, not 445291 specifically.
Self-checkout is conventional automation and is excluded except where new AI changes residual work.
The frozen compensation ratio has a 2024-wage/2022-receipts vintage mismatch and is harmonized from an unadjusted six-digit input.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary from 15-25 eligible 445291 operators, mapped to SOC occupations and separating already-automated POS work, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist demand forecasting, replenishment, promotions, customer inquiries, order capture, bookkeeping, and workforce scheduling, but much of store labor is physical and face-to-face. The estimate is therefore a minority of wage-weighted current tasks rather than a claim that whole retail jobs disappear.

d5.bridge: BLS projects grocery and specialty food retailer employment to decline 1.0% from 2024 to 2034, while separately projecting baker employment to rise about 6% and citing population, income, convenience, and demand for commercial and retail specialty baked goods. Neither measures constant-price quantity at off-premises-made 445291 retailers. I treat those opposing broader signals as roughly flat underlying five-year demand, with downside for channel substitution and upside for population and specialty demand. Employment pressure may understate quantity because productivity can rise; baker growth may overstate this channel because it includes manufacturing and on-premises production.
caveats: BLS projections are employment measures and cover broader populations than 445291 demand.
Constant-quality product mix is difficult to distinguish from premiumization and package-size changes.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or NIQ five-year unit-volume data for packaged baked goods by dedicated specialty-retail channel, adjusted for quality and store openings, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Baked goods are habitual food purchases, supporting stable underlying quantity, but dedicated retailers compete with supermarkets, mass merchants, manufacturer-direct channels, and e-commerce. The midpoint is near flat rather than extrapolating nominal food spending.

caveats: No published source measures the recurring external-customer service share inside 445291.
The frozen n=11 is a margin-bridged estimate from size-class firm counts and average receipts, not an observed EBITDA-band census.
e.change_evidence: A transaction-level sample from 445291 POS systems showing customer type, repeat cadence, contracted or standing-order revenue, and delivery/service components for the 11 frozen LMM-band firms would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The Census definition is retailing off-premises-made baked goods not for immediate consumption. Most such establishments are product retailers serving walk-in consumers and therefore fail the recurring outsourced-service lens; a small subset with meaningful standing B2B, institutional, hospitality, or event accounts can qualify.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 445291 ($704,036,419) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 445291 ($3,005,712,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0540 (margins.json[445]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Grocery and Food) EBITDA/Sales 5.40%); revenue/employee $138,098 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 445291); class <5: 1,266 firms, avg revenue/firm $306,552 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,554 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 464 firms, avg revenue/firm $678,134 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,619 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 343 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,039,262 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $56,120 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 251 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,150,606 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $116,133 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,271,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $338,634 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $107,358,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,797,332 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 11

caveats: The estimate distinguishes elimination of the lens operator from digitization of customer interaction.
A channel shift can preserve baked-goods demand while removing the dedicated 445291 operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and channel-switching data from 445291 account cohorts, including direct-from-manufacturer and grocery-platform migration, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Even with digital ordering and automated administration, customers still need an accountable merchant to select vendors, hold food inventory, manage freshness and recalls, fulfill orders, and resolve exceptions. Some quantity can migrate to grocery platforms, manufacturer-direct distribution, or self-service online channels that eliminate the dedicated retailer.

caveats: No source directly observes benefit sharing following AI implementation in 445291.
The estimate excludes volume loss, which is handled in demand and operator-required share.
q.change_evidence: Five years of gross-margin, price, and customer-retention cohorts from acquired specialty-food retailers, segmented by standing account versus walk-in sales, would test the pass-through assumption.
q.rationale: Fixed product prices let an operator initially retain labor savings, but bakery products are readily compared and customers can switch to grocery, mass retail, direct delivery, or online alternatives. Renewals and competitive repricing should pass through a meaningful share of benefits over five years, while service differentiation and local account relationships preserve some capture.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures actual AI use for all Retail Trade employers, about 14%, and expected use in six months, about 17%, as of spring 2026. I use that current adoption as a floor signal and judge five-year implementation of the narrower exposed task pool, not firm-level tool adoption. Small specialty retailers likely lag larger retailers because of integration cost and weak data, making the sector proxy more likely to overstate near-term implementation; inexpensive embedded POS and accounting tools could instead accelerate diffusion.
caveats: BTOS is sector-wide and measures any AI use, not the share of exposed labor opportunity implemented.
Implementation may improve service or sales without reducing labor, which is not counted here.
rho.change_evidence: Census BTOS microdata cut by NAICS 44529 and employment size, combined with POS-vendor telemetry on enabled AI features and measured labor-hour changes, would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Packaged tools should implement some forecasting, marketing, scheduling, and administrative assistance within five years, while thin margins, fragmented systems, exception handling, data quality, and owner change capacity prevent full conversion of exposed work into avoided labor.

s5.bridge: Project Equity reports 2.9 million US businesses needing buyers over the next decade, a broad owner-succession population rather than 445291 LMM firms or observed control transfers. I translate the decade-scale succession pressure into a five-year qualifying-transfer probability, reduce it for closures, family succession, internal reorganizations, and businesses that are not actually saleable, and widen the range. Because eligible firms may be owner-led local operators, the broad-business proxy may understate their age-driven turnover, but the advocacy framing and distinction between needing and completing a sale may overstate it.
caveats: The source covers all US businesses and a ten-year need-for-buyer concept, not completed five-year control transfers.
Few eligible firms are implied after applying e to the frozen n, so one transaction would change the realized rate sharply.
s5.change_evidence: BizBuySell or DealStats closed-transaction records tagged to 445291, paired with owner-age and succession-plan interviews across the frozen LMM universe, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Owner retirement creates transfer opportunities, but a control transfer within five years is far from certain for a thin-margin specialty retailer; many owners delay, hand off within families, or close rather than sell a transferable operation.

principal_driver: Repeat product demand plus automatable ordering and administrative workflows supports a modest implementable labor opportunity.
principal_weakness: Very few 445291 firms appear eligible because ordinary walk-in product retail is not a recurring outsourced service, leaving a tiny transferable pool.
